basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Ṣād — sūra of the opening letter — begins with an oath “by the Qurʾān, bearer of the Reminder,” in which Qushayrī sees the key to the divine Names: aṣ-Ṣādiq, aṣ-Ṣabūr, aṣ-Ṣamad, aṣ-Ṣāniʿ. It first confronts the pride and schism of the deniers, who marvel that a warner should arise from among them and brand the Prophet a sorcerer, recalling the fate of the annihilated generations — Noah, ʿĀd, Pharaoh “of the stakes,” Thamūd, the people of Lot, the dwellers of the Thicket. Then unfolds the gift of the prophets: David with kingship made firm, endowed with wisdom and decisive speech, tried by the two litigants, who falls down prostrate and turns back; Solomon whom the noble steeds turn for a moment from the remembrance and who returns to his Lord, receiving the wind and the subdued demons; Job, whose patience is rewarded with healing and his family restored; the elect Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ishmael, Elisha, Dhū l-Kifl, endowed with strength and clear sight. It culminates in the great account of Iblīs, who refuses out of pride to prostrate before Adam and falls under the curse. From it Qushayrī draws two pearls: the fault that melts you with regret and brings you back to your Lord profits you more than the obedience whose self-satisfaction draws you away from Him; and worth lies not in the matter one is moulded from, but in divine election — herein Iblīs, weighing fire against clay, was lost through the gaze upon himself. The sūra is sealed upon the universal reminder addressed to the worlds.
themes: the key to the divine Names
the pride and schism of the deniers
David and the trial of repentance
the fall of Iblīs through pride

versets.ar: ص ۚ وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ
versets.tr: ṣād wa-l-qurʾāni dhī dh-dhikri
versets.trad: Ṣād. By the Qurʾān, bearer of the Reminder!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Ṣād is the key to His Names: aṣ-Ṣādiq (the Truthful), aṣ-Ṣabūr (the Patient), aṣ-Ṣamad (the Absolute), aṣ-Ṣāniʿ (the Fashioner). He swears by these realities and by the Qurʾān, bearer of the Reminder. The answer to the oath comes further on: “Truly, this is a certainty: the dispute of the people of the Fire.” It is also said that He swears by the purity of His beloveds’ affection. The nobility of the Qurʾān lies in its being “bearer of the Reminder,” that is, of honour itself.

mots.def: isolated opening letter, key to the divine Names according to Qushayrī.

mots.racine: ق ر أ / ذ ك ر
mots.sens: by the Qurʾān, bearer of the Reminder
mots.def: dhū dh-dhikr: possessed of the Reminder, of honour.

versets.ar: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ
versets.tr: bali lladhīna kafarū fī ʿizzatin wa-shiqāqin
versets.trad: Truly, the deniers are sunk in pride and schism.
versets.commentaire: The deniers are, says Qushayrī, in manifest hardness, declared enmity, a refusal to examine the proofs and to lend an ear to the inner signs. Their “might” (ʿizza) is mere obstinacy, and their “schism” (shiqāq) a breach with the truth that cuts them off from all succour.

mots.def: ʿizza: here, stubborn pride, haughty refusal.

versets.ar: كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ
versets.tr: kam ahlaknā min qablihim min qarnin fa-nādaw wa-lāta ḥīna manāṣin
versets.trad: How many generations We have annihilated before them! All cried out, but the time to escape was past.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they perished when the scourge fell upon them, crying for help — but the time for complaint and for being answered had passed. The cry rises too late; the hour of flight (manāṣ) has closed. A warning to whoever defers his return to God until the door is shut.

versets.ar: وَعَجِبُوا أَن جَاءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ الْكَافِرُونَ هَٰذَا سَاحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ
versets.tr: wa-ʿajibū an jāʾahum mundhirun minhum wa-qāla l-kāfirūna hādhā sāḥirun kadhdhābun
versets.trad: They marvel that a warner from among themselves should come to them. The deniers say: “This is a sorcerer, an arrant liar!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the contradiction: they marvel that a warner should arise from among them, yet do not marvel that carved idols should be gods — a glaring incoherence. Thrown off balance before their prophets, they overwhelm them with the charge of sorcery and share out the slanders, lacking any power to refute the message.

mots.racine: س ح ر / ك ذ ب

versets.ar: أَجَعَلَ الْآلِهَةَ إِلَٰهًا وَاحِدًا ۖ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَشَيْءٌ عُجَابٌ
versets.tr: a-jaʿala l-ālihata ilāhan wāḥidan inna hādhā la-shayʾun ʿujābun
versets.trad: Does he mean to make the deities one single God? This is indeed a strange thing!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the quintessence of Tawḥīd has not so much as brushed their hearts; they are so far from it that they do not even admit its possibility, let alone its affirmation. They have known neither the true God nor the meaning of divinity — for divinity is the power to create from nothing, a power that could not be shared without destroying itself.

mots.racine: أ ل ه / و ح د

versets.ar: وَانطَلَقَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ امْشُوا وَاصْبِرُوا عَلَىٰ آلِهَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَشَيْءٌ يُرَادُ
versets.tr: wa-nṭalaqa l-malaʾu minhum ani mshū wa-ṣbirū ʿalā ālihatikum inna hādhā la-shayʾun yurādu
versets.trad: Their chiefs went off, saying: “Depart, and be faithful to your deities! This is indeed a thing to be desired!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī draws a reversed lesson from their watchword: if the deniers mutually enjoin steadfastness toward their idols, how much more are the believers bound to steadfastness in the worship of their Adored One and to uprightness in their religion. The endurance of the misguided in falsehood shames the lukewarmness of the sincere in the truth.

mots.def: mashā: to walk, to go off.
ṣabara ʿalā: to hold firm, to remain faithful.

versets.ar: مَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَٰذَا فِي الْمِلَّةِ الْآخِرَةِ إِنْ هَٰذَا إِلَّا اخْتِلَاقٌ
versets.tr: mā samiʿnā bi-hādhā fī l-millati l-ākhirati in hādhā illā khtilāqun
versets.trad: We never heard anything of this in the latter religion. This is nothing but a fabrication!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they have rested upon evil and habit, upon the error in which they found their forefathers, and have slumbered in servile imitation and complacency. The “we never heard anything of this” is the confession of a religion inherited without examination, the comfort of conformity set up as an argument.

mots.racine: م ل ل / أ خ ر
mots.def: milla: religious way, community of faith.

versets.ar: أَأُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِن بَيْنِنَا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِّن ذِكْرِي ۖ بَل لَّمَّا يَذُوقُوا عَذَابِ
versets.tr: a-unzila ʿalayhi dh-dhikru min bayninā bal hum fī shakkin min dhikrī bal lammā yadhūqū ʿadhābi
versets.trad: Is it upon this man, taken from among us, that the Reminder has been sent down?” In truth these people are in doubt of My Reminder. They have not yet tasted My chastisement.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: had they possessed clear sight in their religion, they would not have plunged into the excess of their denial; and had We not prolonged for them the blessings and the respite, they would not have given themselves over to their excess. The reprieve is not approval: it is divine wisdom that lets them stray in their blindness, not having yet tasted the chastisement.

mots.racine: ش ك ك / ذ ك ر
ذ و ق / ع ذ ب
mots.sens: they have not tasted My chastisement

versets.ar: أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ
versets.tr: am ʿindahum khazāʾinu raḥmati rabbika l-ʿazīzi l-wahhābi
versets.trad: Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-Mighty, the supreme Bestower?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: these deniers who contest, dispute and give the lie — do they then possess anything of these treasuries? Have they the power to give to whom they will, to favour whom they please, or to climb to heaven and bring down the revelation upon whomever they like? The prophetic grace is a gift of the Bestower (al-Wahhāb), not a property one assigns to oneself.

mots.racine: خ ز ن / ر ح م
mots.sens: the treasuries of your Lord’s mercy

mots.racine: ع ز ز / و ه ب
mots.def: al-Wahhāb: the One who gives without ceasing.

versets.ar: أَمْ لَهُم مُّلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَلْيَرْتَقُوا فِي الْأَسْبَابِ
versets.tr: am lahum mulku s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-mā baynahumā fa-l-yartaqū fī l-asbābi
versets.trad: Do they possess the Kingdom of the heavens, the earth and what is between them? Let them try, then, to climb up by ropes!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: if they claim to dispose of the kingdom, let them then ascend by its means and ways! The challenge is ironic: none possesses the Kingdom save God, and their inability to “climb by the ropes” lays bare the emptiness of their pretension to govern the divine decree.

mots.sens: the kingdom of the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: ر ق ي / س ب ب
mots.sens: let them climb by the means

versets.ar: جُندٌ مَّا هُنَالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِّنَ الْأَحْزَابِ
versets.tr: jundun mā hunālika mahzūmun mina l-aḥzābi
versets.trad: They are but a faction whose hosts shall be routed!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they are but a troop among the confederates, all powerless, doomed to rout. He likens them to the vanquished in their inability to reach their aim: these deniers have neither argument nor strength, and their idols can neither harm nor help, nor defend even themselves.

mots.racine: ه ز م / ح ز ب

versets.ar: كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ ذُو الْأَوْتَادِ
versets.tr: kadhdhabat qablahum qawmu nūḥin wa-ʿādun wa-firʿawnu dhū l-awtādi
versets.trad: Before them the people of Noah, the ʿĀd, and Pharaoh of the stakes cried lies.
themes: the peoples that gave the lie
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes that these peoples are here mentioned together, elsewhere one by one — in each place an added eloquence and a fresh teaching. Pharaoh “of the stakes” (dhū l-awtād) figures a power that thinks itself unshakeable yet collapses before the wonders of Moses: falsehood raised against the messengers inevitably calls down the chastisement.

mots.racine: ق و م / ن و ح

mots.def: awtād: stakes, pillars; emblem of power.

versets.ar: وَثَمُودُ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَأَصْحَابُ الْأَيْكَةِ ۚ أُولَٰئِكَ الْأَحْزَابُ
versets.tr: wa-thamūdu wa-qawmu lūṭin wa-aṣḥābu l-aykati ulāʾika l-aḥzābu
versets.trad: Likewise the Thamūd, the people of Lot, and the dwellers of al-Ayka. These were the factions.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī ranks Thamūd, the people of Lot and the dwellers of the Thicket among “the confederates” (al-aḥzāb) — the lineage of those who league together against the truth down the ages. The enumeration seals the unity of one same refusal: no community has given the lie to its messengers without incurring the promised sanction.

mots.racine: ص ح ب / أ ي ك

mots.def: aḥzāb: factions leagued against the truth.

versets.ar: إِن كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ عِقَابِ
versets.tr: in kullun illā kadhdhaba r-rusula fa-ḥaqqa ʿiqābi
versets.trad: Not one of them but gave the lie to the messengers, and so My chastisement was justly due.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: none of them did otherwise than give the lie to the messengers; thus the chastisement was their due and the punishment deserved. The law is constant: the lie given to God’s messengers makes the sanction “true” (ḥaqqa), that is, inevitably fulfilled.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ر س ل
mots.sens: but to give the lie to the messengers
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to treat as a liar.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ع ق ب

versets.ar: وَمَا يَنظُرُ هَٰؤُلَاءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً مَّا لَهَا مِن فَوَاقٍ
versets.tr: wa-mā yanẓuru hāʾulāʾi illā ṣayḥatan wāḥidatan mā lahā min fawāqin
versets.trad: These await only a single Cry that will have no echo.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they await only the Resurrection, and it will be but a single cry; once raised, it will not subside. The “cry without respite” (fawāq) marks the sudden and final irruption of the Hour, which leaves neither delay nor a chance to draw breath.

mots.racine: ص ي ح / و ح د

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا رَبَّنَا عَجِّل لَّنَا قِطَّنَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْحِسَابِ
versets.tr: wa-qālū rabbanā ʿajjil lanā qiṭṭanā qabla yawmi l-ḥisābi
versets.trad: They say: “Our Lord! Hasten to us our portion before the Day of Reckoning!”
versets.commentaire: In mockery they demand their portion (qiṭṭ) before the Hour. Qushayrī, reading what follows, consoles the Prophet: be patient with their words, for their respite will not last long, and We shall not unduly prolong your trial; soon will descend the help of God and His promise be verified.

mots.racine: ع ج ل / ق ط ط

versets.ar: اصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا دَاوُودَ ذَا الْأَيْدِ ۖ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ
versets.tr: iṣbir ʿalā mā yaqūlūna wa-dhkur ʿabdanā dāwūda dhā l-aydi innahu awwābun
versets.trad: Bear patiently what they say, and remember Our servant David, endowed with strength and ever turning in repentance.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “endowed with strength” (dhū l-ayd) does not denote a proud strength of soul, but a strength of works: David fasted every other day — the most exacting fast — strong in God’s religion through his body, his heart and his resolve. And “awwāb”: one who returns ceaselessly to the obedience and good pleasure of his Lord in every affair — worthy to be taken as a model.

mots.def: awwāb: who returns constantly to God.

versets.ar: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبَالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْرَاقِ
versets.tr: innā sakhkharnā l-jibāla maʿahu yusabbiḥna bi-l-ʿashiyyi wa-l-ishrāqi
versets.trad: We subjected the mountains to glorify Our praises with him at evening and at the rising of the day,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: David glorified, and the mountains glorified with him; he understood their praise, by a grace and a miracle proper to him. And he adds the spiritual law: whoever realizes himself fully in his state, all that draws near to him aids him, and even what is foreign to him comes under his influence. The morning (ishrāq) is, according to Ibn ʿAbbās, the hour of the prayer of brightness.

mots.racine: س خ ر / ج ب ل
mots.def: sakhkhara: to subjugate, to set to service.

mots.racine: ع ش و / ش ر ق
mots.sens: at evening and at the rising of day
mots.def: ishrāq: brightness of morning, solar radiance.

versets.ar: وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُ أَوَّابٌ
versets.tr: wa-ṭ-ṭayra maḥshūratan kullun lahu awwābun
versets.trad: and the birds gathered about him. All things turn toward God.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the birds would gather near him and glorify God, and David knew their praise. “All turn toward Him” (kull lahu awwāb): every creature, in its own way, returns and responds to its Lord. He illustrates this communion through the poet’s verses, where the turtledove weeping for its nest and the lover weeping for his beloved understand one another by the sole language of grief.

mots.racine: ط ي ر / ح ش ر

mots.def: awwāb: who returns, turns to God.

versets.ar: وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُ وَآتَيْنَاهُ الْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ الْخِطَابِ
versets.tr: wa-shadadnā mulkahu wa-ātaynāhu l-ḥikmata wa-faṣla l-khiṭābi
versets.trad: We made his kingship firm, and We gave him wisdom and the art of decisive speech.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī multiplies the senses of “We made his kingship firm”: by Our succour and the warding off of misfortune; by justice in judgement and good conduct toward his subjects; by breaking the hand of the oppressors; by the prayer of the oppressed; by his vigilance and his art of governing; by his acceptance of the truth from whoever brought it; and by his constant return to Us at every moment. “Wisdom” is rectitude and discernment; “decisive speech” (faṣl al-khiṭāb), judgement according to the truth.

mots.racine: ش د د / م ل ك
ف ص ل / خ ط ب
mots.def: faṣl al-khiṭāb: trenchant judgement, decisive word.

versets.ar: وَهَلْ أَتَاكَ نَبَأُ الْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا الْمِحْرَابَ
versets.tr: wa-hal atāka nabaʾu l-khaṣmi idh tasawwarū l-miḥrāba
versets.trad: Has the story of the litigants reached you? They climbed into the sanctuary
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God sent David two angels in the guise of two men, who scaled the sanctuary to admonish him. The account opens David’s trial — a willed trial, not a grave sin, according to those who preserve the prophets from all fault; what matters is less the cause than the return it awakens.

mots.racine: ن ب أ / خ ص م
س و ر / ح ر ب

versets.ar: إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَىٰ دَاوُودَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ قَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ ۖ خَصْمَانِ بَغَىٰ بَعْضُنَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَاحْكُم بَيْنَنَا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَا تُشْطِطْ وَاهْدِنَا إِلَىٰ سَوَاءِ الصِّرَاطِ
versets.tr: idh dakhalū ʿalā dāwūda fa-faziʿa minhum qālū lā takhaf khaṣmāni baghā baʿḍunā ʿalā baʿḍin fa-ḥkum baynanā bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-lā tushṭiṭ wa-hdinā ilā sawāʾi ṣ-ṣirāṭi
versets.trad: and reached David, who took fright at them. “Fear not!” they said. “We are two litigants, one of whom has wronged the other. Judge between us in truth, without partiality, and guide us to the path of uprightness.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī relates that David, that day, had shut himself in for worship, bolted his door and posted his guards: he closed the door upon himself, but could not close the door of heaven; he ordered the people kept away, but none could keep from him the decree of judgement. And he cites the sages’ maxim: whoever flees what must come to pass still struggles in the palm of the One who pursues him.

mots.def: faziʿa: to be seized with fear.
shaṭaṭa: to be unjust, to exceed measure.

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذَا أَخِي لَهُ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَلِيَ نَعْجَةٌ وَاحِدَةٌ فَقَالَ أَكْفِلْنِيهَا وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطَابِ
versets.tr: inna hādhā akhī lahu tisʿun wa-tisʿūna naʿjatan wa-liya naʿjatun wāḥidatun fa-qāla akfilnīhā wa-ʿazzanī fī l-khiṭābi
versets.trad: “This is my brother: he had ninety-nine ewes, and I had but a single ewe. He said to me one day: ‘Entrust her to me,’ and he got the better of me in the dispute!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī glosses: “entrust her to me” (akfilnīhā): cede her to me that I may have charge of her; “he got the better of me in the debate” (ʿazzanī): he overcame me by speech. Under the veil of the ewe is told, by delicate allusion, a situation in which the stronger presses the weaker — a mirror held up to David that he might judge himself.

mots.def: akfala: to commit into charge, into keeping.

mots.racine: ع ز ز / خ ط ب
mots.sens: he got the better of me in debate

versets.ar: قَالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤَالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلَىٰ نِعَاجِهِ ۖ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ الْخُلَطَاءِ لَيَبْغِي بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ ۗ وَظَنَّ دَاوُودُ أَنَّمَا فَتَنَّاهُ فَاسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُ وَخَرَّ رَاكِعًا وَأَنَابَ
versets.tr: qāla la-qad ẓalamaka bi-suʾāli naʿjatika ilā niʿājihi wa-inna kathīran mina l-khulaṭāʾi la-yabghī baʿḍuhum ʿalā baʿḍin illā lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti wa-qalīlun mā hum wa-ẓanna dāwūdu annamā fatannāhu fa-staghfara rabbahu wa-kharra rākiʿan wa-anāba
versets.trad: David said: “He has wronged you in asking for your ewe to add it to his own.” Indeed, many partners wrong one another, save those who believe and do righteous deeds; but these are few! David understood that We had only meant to try him. He asked forgiveness of his Lord, fell down prostrate and repented.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: David then knew that it was a warning and a reproach for what had been; certain that the promised trial had reached him, he gave himself over to imploring. He relates that David remained prostrate, weeping until grass sprang from his tears, and drew from it two pearls: whoever returns to God at the first onset of trial, God suffices him; and the fault that melts you with regret and draws you near to your Lord profits you more than the obedience whose complacency draws you away from Him.

mots.racine: خ ر ر / ر ك ع / ن و ب
mots.sens: he fell bowing down and turned to God
mots.def: anāba: to repent, to return to God.
fatana: to put to the test.

versets.ar: فَغَفَرْنَا لَهُ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَهُ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ
versets.tr: fa-ghafarnā lahu dhālika wa-inna lahu ʿindanā la-zulfā wa-ḥusna maʾābin
versets.trad: We forgave him, and he has with Us a place of nearness and a beautiful place of return.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: he has with Us a nearness (zulfā) and a beautiful return. And he draws from it the hope of sinners: when David had recourse, at the very start of the trial, to repentance, to tears, to humble entreaty, he found forgiveness and indulgence — so it is with whoever returns to God at the first distresses: God spares him what threatens him.

mots.racine: ح س ن / أ و ب
mots.def: maʾāb: place of return, final abode.

versets.ar: يَا دَاوُودُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَاكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُم بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِمَا نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسَابِ
versets.tr: yā dāwūdu innā jaʿalnāka khalīfatan fī l-arḍi fa-ḥkum bayna n-nāsi bi-l-ḥaqqi wa-lā tattabiʿi l-hawā fa-yuḍillaka ʿan sabīli llāhi inna lladhīna yaḍillūna ʿan sabīli llāhi lahum ʿadhābun shadīdun bi-mā nasū yawma l-ḥisābi
versets.trad: O David! We have made you a vicegerent on the earth: judge among men in truth; follow not desire, for it would lead you astray from the path of God. Those who stray from the path of God shall suffer a terrible chastisement for having forgotten the Day of Reckoning.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “We have made you a vicegerent” — successor of the prophets who preceded you; judge in My name among My servants according to the truth. And the counsel not to follow desire (hawā) signals that the gravest misdeed of the servant and his ugliest sin is to follow his inclination: desire is the root of straying from the path of God.

mots.racine: خ ل ف / أ ر ض
ت ب ع / ه و ي

versets.ar: وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَاطِلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ
versets.tr: wa-mā khalaqnā s-samāʾa wa-l-arḍa wa-mā baynahumā bāṭilan dhālika ẓannu lladhīna kafarū fa-waylun li-lladhīna kafarū mina n-nāri
versets.trad: We did not create the heaven, the earth and what is between them in vain play, as the deniers suppose. Woe to the deniers, for whom awaits the Fire!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: I did not create them in vain, nor through falsehood: My act has a reason, and I am in the right in creating them; I did not create them for nothingness, but to order their affair through truth. To believe creation gratuitous is the conjecture of the deniers; the wise reads in it, on the contrary, the call to responsibility before the Day of Reckoning.

versets.ar: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ
versets.tr: am najʿalu lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti ka-l-mufsidīna fī l-arḍi am najʿalu l-muttaqīna ka-l-fujjāri
versets.trad: Shall We treat those who believe and do righteous deeds like those who spread corruption on the earth? Or shall We treat those who fear God like the wicked?
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God will never equate the corrupters with the doers of good. The question is rhetorical and decisive: it would be contrary to divine wisdom to confound the lot of the believer working good with that of the sower of corruption, the God-fearing (muttaqī) with the dissolute (fājir). The distinction of ends reveals the seriousness of creation.

mots.sens: like the corrupters on the earth

mots.racine: و ق ي / ف ج ر

versets.ar: كِتَابٌ أَنزَلْنَاهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَارَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوا آيَاتِهِ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ
versets.tr: kitābun anzalnāhu ilayka mubārakun li-yaddabbarū āyātihi wa-li-yatadhakkara ulū l-albābi
versets.trad: This is a blessed Book, which We have sent down upon you that men may meditate its verses, and that those endowed with understanding may reflect.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds the sense of “blessed” (mubārak): of immense profit; lasting and permanent, which no Book abrogates; blessed for whoever believes in it and bears witness to it. Then he specifies: the blessing is in its meditation and in reflection upon its meanings — not in mere recitation, but in the tadabbur that opens its depths to those “endowed with heart.”

mots.def: mubārak: bearer of baraka, of fruitfulness.

mots.racine: د ب ر / أ ي ي
mots.sens: that they may meditate its signs

versets.ar: وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُودَ سُلَيْمَانَ ۚ نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ
versets.tr: wa-wahabnā li-dāwūda sulaymāna niʿma l-ʿabdu innahu awwābun
versets.trad: To David We gave Solomon: what an excellent servant, ever turning in repentance!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “what an excellent servant” — for he was “awwāb,” returning to God in all his states: in blessing through gratitude, in trial through patience. Solomon, like David his father, embodies the servant whose heart turns back to God whatever befalls, ease as well as affliction.

mots.racine: ن ع م / ع ب د
mots.def: niʿma: formula of praise; ʿabd: servant.
awwāb: who turns back to God.

versets.ar: إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِالْعَشِيِّ الصَّافِنَاتُ الْجِيَادُ
versets.tr: idh ʿuriḍa ʿalayhi bi-l-ʿashiyyi ṣ-ṣāfinātu l-jiyādu
versets.trad: When, one evening, the noble steeds were paraded before him,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the “ṣāfināt” are the mares that stand poised on three legs, raising one hoof — thoroughbred horses. According to the accounts, a great number were paraded before Solomon, “in the evening” (bi-l-ʿashiyy), at the decline of day, and their contemplation distracts him for a moment from some of his litanies of remembrance.

mots.def: ṣāfina: mare standing still on three legs.

versets.ar: فَقَالَ إِنِّي أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ الْخَيْرِ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّي حَتَّىٰ تَوَارَتْ بِالْحِجَابِ
versets.tr: fa-qāla innī aḥbabtu ḥubba l-khayri ʿan dhikri rabbī ḥattā tawārat bi-l-ḥijābi
versets.trad: He cried: “Behold, I have preferred the love of [worldly] goods to the remembrance of my Lord!” [and he stayed gazing at them] until the mares disappeared behind the veil [of darkness].
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reads Solomon’s confession as a moment when attachment to goods (ḥubb al-khayr) eclipses the remembrance of God — “until they were veiled.” But he at once makes of it a lesson in detachment, anticipating what follows: whoever forsakes a thing for God loses nothing of it in God’s sight, for the loss consented to opens onto a higher gift.

mots.racine: ح ب ب / خ ي ر
mots.sens: I have loved the love of goods
mots.def: al-khayr: here, the horses, the goods.

mots.racine: و ر ي / ح ج ب
mots.def: tawārā: to slip away, to hide.

versets.ar: رُدُّوهَا عَلَيَّ ۖ فَطَفِقَ مَسْحًا بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاقِ
versets.tr: ruddūhā ʿalayya fa-ṭafiqa masḥan bi-s-sūqi wa-l-aʿnāqi
versets.trad: “Bring them back to me!” he then said, and he began to stroke their legs and necks.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports the views: he began to pass his hand over their legs and necks, as a sign of attention, after completing his prayer; according to others, he dedicated them to God. Whatever the case — he notes — all this was lawful in his Law. The gesture marks the recovery of self after the passing distraction.

mots.def: radda: to give back, to bring back.

mots.racine: م س ح / س و ق / ع ن ق

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا سُلَيْمَانَ وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَىٰ كُرْسِيِّهِ جَسَدًا ثُمَّ أَنَابَ
versets.tr: wa-la-qad fatannā sulaymāna wa-alqaynā ʿalā kursiyyihi jasadan thumma anāba
versets.trad: We indeed tried Solomon by placing upon his throne a [lifeless] body; thereafter he turned back in repentance.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports several accounts of this trial, but draws out the spiritual sense: in one affair Solomon had neglected to commit his lot to God — he had spoken without the “if God wills,” or had leaned too much on a help other than His. The trial lay in this leaning outside of God; better would have been trusting surrender (tawakkul). And he concludes: “thereafter he turned back” — the fault corrected by return is worth more than the work spoiled by self-satisfaction.

mots.def: fatana: to put to the test.
anāba: to repent, to turn back.

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَهَبْ لِي مُلْكًا لَّا يَنبَغِي لِأَحَدٍ مِّن بَعْدِي ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْوَهَّابُ
versets.tr: qāla rabbi ghfir lī wa-hab lī mulkan lā yanbaghī li-aḥadin min baʿdī innaka anta l-wahhābu
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! Forgive me, and grant me a kingdom such that none after me may possess its like. You, truly, are the supreme Bestower!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī elevates Solomon’s request: he asked not for the outward kingdom, but to possess himself — for the true king is the one who is master of his soul, and whoever masters himself no longer follows his desire. He sees in it too: the request for a state of total contemplation of his Lord, where he sees none other than Him; the contentment (qanāʿa) in which no choice of one’s own remains.

mots.racine: و ه ب / م ل ك

mots.def: al-Wahhāb: the One who gives without measure.

versets.ar: فَسَخَّرْنَا لَهُ الرِّيحَ تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ رُخَاءً حَيْثُ أَصَابَ
versets.tr: fa-sakhkharnā lahu r-rīḥa tajrī bi-amrihi rukhāʾan ḥaythu aṣāba
versets.trad: Then We subjected to him the wind, which by his command blew gently wherever he directed it,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī links this to the forsaken horses: God accepted his effort and subjected the wind to him in exchange for the steeds — with no more need of fodder or care. And this is more eloquent still: whoever forsakes a thing for God loses nothing of it in His sight, but receives a gift of a higher nature.

mots.racine: س خ ر / ر و ح
mots.sens: We subjected to him the wind

mots.racine: ر خ و / ص و ب
mots.def: rukhāʾ: a light and docile breath.

versets.ar: وَالشَّيَاطِينَ كُلَّ بَنَّاءٍ وَغَوَّاصٍ
versets.tr: wa-sh-shayāṭīna kulla bannāʾin wa-ghawwāṣin
versets.trad: and also the demons, builders and divers,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: just as We subjected the wind to him, We subjected the demons to him — builders and divers, set to the service of his mission. Solomon’s dominion over the rebellious forces marks the authority of the servant whom God has set aright after the trial.

versets.ar: وَآخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفَادِ
versets.tr: wa-ākharīna muqarranīna fī l-aṣfādi
versets.trad: and others bound together in chains:
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī ranks these other demons “shackled in chains” (muqarranīn fī l-aṣfād) among the subjected forces: the rebels themselves are bound and mastered. The image shows that nothing escapes the power conferred by God upon His grateful servant.

versets.ar: هَٰذَا عَطَاؤُنَا فَامْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ
versets.tr: hādhā ʿaṭāʾunā fa-mnun aw amsik bi-ghayri ḥisābin
versets.trad: “This is Our gift: either bestow it or withhold it, with no account to render!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “This is Our gift” — give or withhold, keep it; there is no reckoning upon you. He then digresses on the gift granted: to walk in the air and cross distances in a short time assuredly exists in this Community by grace; and that such gifts were conferred upon the servants of the Prophet, in honour of him, proves the superior eminence of his rank.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ لَهُ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ
versets.tr: wa-inna lahu ʿindanā la-zulfā wa-ḥusna maʾābin
versets.trad: Yes, Solomon has with Us a place of nearness and a beautiful place of return.
versets.commentaire: The same promise as to David seals the account of Solomon: a nearness (zulfā) and a beautiful return with Us. Qushayrī thus stresses that the trial undergone and the return accomplished do not diminish the prophet’s rank, but confirm it in divine favour.

mots.racine: ح س ن / أ و ب

versets.ar: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطَانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذَابٍ
versets.tr: wa-dhkur ʿabdanā ayyūba idh nādā rabbahu annī massaniya sh-shayṭānu bi-nuṣbin wa-ʿadhābin
versets.trad: Mention too Our servant Job, when he called upon his Lord: “Satan has overwhelmed me with suffering and torment!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Satan touched him through his insinuations, ceaselessly reminding him of the misfortune that overwhelmed him. He reports several causes of Job’s trial, but the phrase “Satan has touched me” aims only at the torment of temptation, not a complaint against God: Job names the agent of the disturbance without contesting the decree.

mots.racine: ن د و / ر ب ب
ن ص ب / ع ذ ب

versets.ar: ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ ۖ هَٰذَا مُغْتَسَلٌ بَارِدٌ وَشَرَابٌ
versets.tr: urkuḍ bi-rijlika hādhā mughtasalun bāridun wa-sharābun
versets.trad: “Stamp with your foot! Here is cool water to wash with and to drink.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when God willed to lift Job’s trial, He said to him: “Stamp with your foot”; he stamped, and there gushed forth a spring of cool water with which he washed — and his beauty and wholeness returned to him. The slight gesture opens, by divine command, the way to a total restoration of body and life.

mots.racine: ر ك ض / ر ج ل
mots.def: rakaḍa: to stamp the foot, to tread.

mots.racine: غ س ل / ب ر د
mots.def: mughtasal: water or place for washing.

versets.ar: وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ
versets.tr: wa-wahabnā lahu ahlahu wa-mithlahum maʿahum raḥmatan minnā wa-dhikrā li-ulī l-albābi
versets.trad: We restored to him his family, and doubled their number, as a mercy from Us and a reminder for those endowed with understanding.
versets.commentaire: The gift exceeds mere reparation: the family restored, doubled, by “mercy from Us” (raḥma minnā). Qushayrī sees in it a “reminder for those endowed with heart”: the outcome of Job’s trial teaches that patience under the decree draws down a grace that surpasses what was taken away.

mots.racine: و ه ب / أ ه ل
mots.sens: We restored to him his family

versets.ar: وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثًا فَاضْرِب بِّهِ وَلَا تَحْنَثْ ۗ إِنَّا وَجَدْنَاهُ صَابِرًا ۚ نِّعْمَ الْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ
versets.tr: wa-khudh bi-yadika ḍighthan fa-ḍrib bihi wa-lā taḥnath innā wajadnāhu ṣābiran niʿma l-ʿabdu innahu awwābun
versets.trad: Take in your hand a bundle of straw and strike with it; break not your oath! Indeed, We found him patient! What an excellent servant, ever turning in repentance!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī defines Job’s patience: patience is not to oppose the decree; it is to stand beneath the judgement, to savour the trial rather than find it bitter, to abide with God in beautiful courtesy. And “what an excellent servant”: the trial did not distract him from the One who tries; he came out of the affliction such as he had entered it. The bundle of straw allows the oath to be kept without cruel rigour.

mots.racine: و ج د / ص ب ر

versets.ar: وَاذْكُرْ عِبَادَنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ أُولِي الْأَيْدِي وَالْأَبْصَارِ
versets.tr: wa-dhkur ʿibādanā ibrāhīma wa-isḥāqa wa-yaʿqūba ulī l-aydī wa-l-abṣāri
versets.trad: Mention Our servants Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, endowed with strength and clear sight.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “endowed with hands” (ulī l-aydī) means endowed with strength; “and with eyes” (al-abṣār), with inner clear-sightedness. Strength in action and light in discernment: such are the two wings of the elect, who join the vigour of the work to the penetration of the heart.

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَخْلَصْنَاهُم بِخَالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ
versets.tr: innā akhlaṣnāhum bi-khāliṣatin dhikrā d-dāri
versets.trad: We purified them with a pure quality: the remembrance of the eternal Abode.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives several senses: We set them apart by a pure quality — the remembrance of the Garden and the Fire, or the calling of men to the Garden and flight from the Fire; or rather: by a heart freed from the remembrance of the two abodes, acting without regard to recompense; they stripped themselves bare for Us, the heart liberated from concern for the Abode. Election is purification of the motive as much as of the act.

mots.def: akhlaṣa: to make pure, to set apart.

mots.racine: ذ ك ر / د و ر

versets.ar: وَإِنَّهُمْ عِندَنَا لَمِنَ الْمُصْطَفَيْنَ الْأَخْيَارِ
versets.tr: wa-innahum ʿindanā la-mina l-muṣṭafayna l-akhyāri
versets.trad: They are with Us among the elect, the best.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī confirms their rank: they are, with God, among the elect (al-muṣṭafayn), the best (al-akhyār). The election (iṣṭifāʾ) crowns the purification of the preceding verse: chosen and held to be the most excellent, not by their merit alone, but by the favour of the One who elects.

versets.ar: وَاذْكُرْ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ ۖ وَكُلٌّ مِّنَ الْأَخْيَارِ
versets.tr: wa-dhkur ismāʿīla wa-l-yasaʿa wa-dhā l-kifli wa-kullun mina l-akhyāri
versets.trad: Mention Ishmael, Elisha (al-Yasaʿ) and Ezekiel (Dhū l-Kifl): all are among the best.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī reports concerning Dhū l-Kifl: he is said to have stood surety (kafala) before God for the work of a righteous man who died in his time; or else he took charge of a hundred men of the Children of Israel fleeing an unjust prince and provided for their sustenance. All — Ishmael, Elisha, Dhū l-Kifl — are counted among “the best” (al-akhyār).

versets.ar: هَٰذَا ذِكْرٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ
versets.tr: hādhā dhikrun wa-inna li-l-muttaqīna la-ḥusna maʾābin
versets.trad: This is a reminder: for those who fear God a beautiful place of return is appointed,
themes: the beautiful end of the God-fearing
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “this is a reminder” — this Qurʾān contains the account of what was, of the prophets and the stories; and it is an honour for you, O Prophet, for this miracle attests your truthfulness. And to the God-fearing who guard against disobedience is promised “a beautiful return” (ḥusn maʾāb), the most beautiful of ends.

mots.racine: ح س ن / أ و ب

versets.ar: جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ الْأَبْوَابُ
versets.tr: jannāti ʿadnin mufattaḥatan lahumu l-abwābu
versets.trad: the gardens of Eden whose gates stand open for them.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: when they come to them, no humiliation of being held at the gate shall reach them, no embarrassment of asking leave; the angels will receive them with welcome and deference. The open gates (mufattaḥa) tell of the grace of free access, without the “veil” or the waiting that weigh upon the excluded.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ع د ن
ف ت ح / ب و ب

versets.ar: مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيهَا يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِفَاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَشَرَابٍ
versets.tr: muttakiʾīna fīhā yadʿūna fīhā bi-fākihatin kathīratin wa-sharābin
versets.trad: Reclining therein, they shall call for abundant fruits and drink
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī paints the serenity of the elect: reclining upon their couches of honour, they call therein for abundant fruits and drink at their pleasure. The reclining (ittikāʾ) figures the perfect repose of the accepted servant, to whom every desire is granted without toil or delay.

mots.racine: ف ك ه / ك ث ر / ش ر ب

versets.ar: وَعِندَهُمْ قَاصِرَاتُ الطَّرْفِ أَتْرَابٌ
versets.tr: wa-ʿindahum qāṣirātu ṭ-ṭarfi atrābun
versets.trad: while beside them shall be those of modest gaze, all of the same age.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: beside them, houris “of restrained gaze” (qāṣirāt aṭ-ṭarf), that is, turned away from all but their spouses; “atrāb”: of the same age, equal in beauty, grace and form. The purity of the gaze expresses the perfect fidelity of the promised felicity.

mots.racine: ق ص ر / ط ر ف
mots.def: qāṣirāt aṭ-ṭarf: who lower the gaze, chaste.

versets.ar: هَٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ الْحِسَابِ
versets.tr: hādhā mā tūʿadūna li-yawmi l-ḥisābi
versets.trad: This is what is promised to you for the Day of Reckoning.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī ties this promise to the “Day of Reckoning”: it is the recompense reserved for the God-fearing, the fulfilment of what was announced to them. The felicity described is not a dream, but a word of God guaranteed for the appointed term.

mots.sens: this is what is promised to you

versets.ar: إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَرِزْقُنَا مَا لَهُ مِن نَّفَادٍ
versets.tr: inna hādhā la-rizqunā mā lahu min nafādin
versets.trad: Such are the gifts We bestow upon you, and they shall never be exhausted.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī stresses the inexhaustible character of this provision (rizq): “it knows no exhaustion” (mā lahu min nafād). Unlike the goods of this lower world that wear out and come to an end, the gift of the Garden abides, sign of the limitless generosity of the Bestower.

versets.ar: هَٰذَا ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلطَّاغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَآبٍ
versets.tr: hādhā wa-inna li-ṭ-ṭāghīna la-sharra maʾābin
versets.trad: So it shall be. But for the rebels the place of return shall be hateful:
versets.commentaire: After the felicity, the contrast: to the “rebels” (ṭāghīn) belongs “the worst of returns” (sharr maʾāb). Qushayrī marks the Qurʾānic balancing between the two ends — the beautiful issue of the God-fearing, the sinister abode of the transgressors — that the soul may choose in full knowledge.

mots.racine: ش ر ر / أ و ب
mots.def: sharr: the worst; maʾāb: abode of return.

versets.ar: جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا فَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ
versets.tr: jahannama yaṣlawnahā fa-biʾsa l-mihādu
versets.trad: it shall be Gehenna, in which they shall burn. What a hateful resting place!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: it is Gehenna; they enter it and abide therein chastised — what a hateful abode! The “couch” (mihād), which ought to be repose, here becomes a place of torment: bitter irony of the lot of the transgressors whose bed is the Fire.

mots.def: ṣaliya: to be exposed to fire, to roast in it.

versets.ar: هَٰذَا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ
versets.tr: hādhā fa-l-yadhūqūhu ḥamīmun wa-ghassāqun
versets.trad: So it shall be. Let them taste, then, a boiling water, a fetid fluid
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the “ḥamīm” is the scalding water, and the “ghassāq” the exudate of the damned — according to others, the icy cold of Gehenna that burns by its bite as boiling water burns by its heat. The chastisement unites the extremes, the furnace and the frost, that no respite may soften the pain.

mots.def: ḥamīm: liquid brought to the boil.
ghassāq: pus, icy exudate of the damned.

versets.ar: وَآخَرُ مِن شَكْلِهِ أَزْوَاجٌ
versets.tr: wa-ākharu min shaklihi azwājun
versets.trad: and other torments of the same kind.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: yet other sorts, of the same kind as this chastisement. The expression “in pairs” (azwāj) signifies the multiplicity of paired torments: to each torment answers another like it, without the retribution of the transgressors being exhausted.

versets.ar: هَٰذَا فَوْجٌ مُّقْتَحِمٌ مَّعَكُمْ ۖ لَا مَرْحَبًا بِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ صَالُو النَّارِ
versets.tr: hādhā fawjun muqtaḥimun maʿakum lā marḥaban bihim innahum ṣālū n-nāri
versets.trad: Here is a throng plunging in after the rebels. “No welcome for you,” they are told; “you shall burn in the Fire.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: here is a troop rushing into the Fire with you, these are your followers. The dialogue opens between leaders and disciples: “no welcome” — the reverse of the reception reserved for the elect. Hell returns to the damned the contrary of the honours done to the people of the Garden.

mots.racine: ف و ج / ق ح م
mots.def: iqtaḥama: to plunge, to rush in.

versets.ar: قَالُوا بَلْ أَنتُمْ لَا مَرْحَبًا بِكُمْ ۖ أَنتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرَارُ
versets.tr: qālū bal antum lā marḥaban bikum antum qaddamtumūhu lanā fa-biʾsa l-qarāru
versets.trad: “No welcome for you [either],” say [to the rebels] these latecomers! “It is you who prepared this for us, and a hateful abode it is!”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the followers answer the leaders: “no welcome to you! It is you who advanced this to us by your command, and we followed you.” The mutual recrimination reveals the solidarity in error become mutual accusation in the chastisement — “what a hateful abode!”

mots.sens: it is you who prepared it for us

versets.ar: قَالُوا رَبَّنَا مَن قَدَّمَ لَنَا هَٰذَا فَزِدْهُ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا فِي النَّارِ
versets.tr: qālū rabbanā man qaddama lanā hādhā fa-zidhu ʿadhāban ḍiʿfan fī n-nāri
versets.trad: They also say: “Our Lord! For him who prepared this for us, double the chastisement in the Fire.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: they say: “Lord, whoever advanced this to us, double for him the chastisement in the Fire” — but they are answered: you are all in it, and the chastisement shall be lightened for none of you. The vengeance asked for falls back upon the one who asks: none is exempted in the common perdition.

mots.racine: ز ي د / ض ع ف

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ الْأَشْرَارِ
versets.tr: wa-qālū mā lanā lā narā rijālan kunnā naʿudduhum mina l-ashrāri
versets.trad: And they ask: “How is it that we do not see here the men we counted among the worst of creatures?
themes: the absence of the mocked believers
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the deniers, entering the Fire, say: “why do we not see men whom we counted in the world among the wicked and the lowly?” He sees in it Abū Jahl and his like seeking Bilāl and the humble; they are told that these are in the Firdaws — and their regrets are redoubled.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ر ج ل
mots.sens: why do we not see men

versets.ar: أَتَّخَذْنَاهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زَاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ الْأَبْصَارُ
versets.tr: attakhadhnāhum sikhriyyan am zāghat ʿanhumu l-abṣāru
versets.trad: Did we take them [wrongly] as an object of ridicule, or have they escaped our sight?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī continues the dialogue: “did we wrongly take them as an object of mockery, or have our eyes missed them?” The mockery of old turns back upon the mockers: those they despised are in Paradise, and their present blindness prolongs the blindness of their past arrogance.

mots.racine: أ خ ذ / س خ ر
mots.sens: did we take them in mockery

mots.racine: ز ي غ / ب ص ر
mots.sens: or have our eyes missed them

versets.ar: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ
versets.tr: inna dhālika la-ḥaqqun takhāṣumu ahli n-nāri
versets.trad: Truly, this dispute among the people of the Fire is a reality!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the dispute of the people of the Fire, within the Fire, is a certain truth. This is the answer to the opening oath (“Ṣād. By the Qurʾān…”): the quarrel of the damned is indeed real, sealing the warning with which the sūra had begun.

mots.racine: خ ص م / ن و ر
mots.sens: the dispute of the people of the Fire

versets.ar: قُلْ إِنَّمَا أَنَا مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا اللَّهُ الْوَاحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ
versets.tr: qul innamā anā mundhirun wa-mā min ilāhin illā llāhu l-wāḥidu l-qahhāru
versets.trad: Say: “I am only a warner. There is no deity but God, the One, the Invincible,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: say, O Muḥammad: I am only a warner who conveys the message of my Lord; and there is no god but God, the One without partner. The Prophet’s role comes down to inḏār — to warn — and the heart of the message to Tawḥīd: the One (al-Wāḥid), the Subduer (al-Qahhār) to whom nothing resists.

mots.racine: و ح د / ق ه ر
mots.def: al-Qahhār: the One who dominates all.

versets.ar: رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ
versets.tr: rabbu s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-mā baynahumā l-ʿazīzu l-ghaffāru
versets.trad: Lord of the heavens and the earth and what is between them, the All-Mighty, the One who forgives generously.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī here unites the two faces of the Name: al-ʿAzīz, the invincible Mighty, and al-Ghaffār, the One who covers and forgives without ceasing. The universal lordship over the heavens and the earth is accompanied by a mercy that veils faults: majesty does not exclude clemency, it grounds it.

mots.sens: Lord of the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: ع ز ز / غ ف ر

versets.ar: قُلْ هُوَ نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ
versets.tr: qul huwa nabaʾun ʿaẓīmun
versets.trad: Say: “It is a solemn tiding,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: what I bring you — the tiding of the Resurrection and the Gathering, of the Garden and the Fire, and what I tell you of my prophethood and my truthfulness — is an immense tiding (nabaʾ ʿaẓīm). The gravity of the message lies in its object: the final destiny of every soul.

mots.racine: ن ب أ / ع ظ م

mots.def: pronoun referring back to the message.

versets.ar: أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ
versets.tr: antum ʿanhu muʿriḍūna
versets.trad: yet you turn away from it…
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: and you turn away from it (muʿriḍūn). The immensity of the tiding makes graver still the blindness of whoever turns from it: before the announcement of the Day, indifference is not neutrality but refusal, and the turning away seals the responsibility.

mots.def: aʿraḍa: to turn away, to lose interest.

versets.ar: مَا كَانَ لِيَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلَىٰ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ
versets.tr: mā kāna liya min ʿilmin bi-l-malaʾi l-aʿlā idh yakhtaṣimūna
versets.trad: I have no knowledge of the supreme assembly and the disputes they hold among themselves.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: I would have no knowledge of the sublime Assembly (al-malaʾ al-aʿlā) and its debate had God not taught it me. The assembly is that of the angels, and their “dispute” concerned the creation of Adam — “will You place therein one who will sow corruption?” He recalls the ḥadīth in which the angels’ debate concerned the degrees and the expiations: a difference over the excellence of works, not a negation.

mots.racine: م ل أ / ع ل و
mots.def: al-malaʾ al-aʿlā: the angels brought near.

versets.ar: إِن يُوحَىٰ إِلَيَّ إِلَّا أَنَّمَا أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: in yūḥā ilayya illā annamā anā nadhīrun mubīnun
versets.trad: It is revealed to me only because I am a clear warner.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: this is revealed to me only because I am a clear warner (nadhīr mubīn). The knowledge of the Unseen comes to him only by revelation, and in the service of his function: to warn clearly. The Prophet claims no knowledge of his own, but receives what serves the reminder.

mots.sens: it is revealed to me only

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / ب ي ن

versets.ar: إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي خَالِقٌ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ
versets.tr: idh qāla rabbuka li-l-malāʾikati innī khāliqun basharan min ṭīnin
versets.trad: So it came to pass that your Lord said to the angels: “Yes, I shall create a mortal from clay.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: that He should inform the angels of it attests Adam’s greatness: God created the two worlds, the Garden and the Fire, the Throne and the Footstool, the angels — and of none of them did He say what He said of Adam. And he marvels: glory be to Him! He created the noblest of His creatures from the vilest of things — earth and clay.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ب ش ر

versets.ar: فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ سَاجِدِينَ
versets.tr: fa-idhā sawwaytuhu wa-nafakhtu fīhi min rūḥī fa-qaʿū lahu sājidīna
versets.trad: When I have shaped him harmoniously and breathed into him of My Spirit, fall down prostrate before him.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the soul of Adam, though created, has over the other spirits an honour, for it alone is named by “My Spirit.” When He completed his form and set the spirit within him, He clothed him in the lights of election, and his prestige fell upon the angels, who prostrated at His command. The breathing of the rūḥ confers upon man his sacred dignity.

mots.racine: ن ف خ / ر و ح
mots.sens: I breathed into him of My Spirit

versets.ar: فَسَجَدَ الْمَلَائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ
versets.tr: fa-sajada l-malāʾikatu kulluhum ajmaʿūna
versets.trad: The angels prostrated all together,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī stresses the totality of the act: the angels prostrated, all without exception — an immediate response to the divine command and a recognition of the dignity conferred upon Adam. Their prostration is not worship of Adam, but obedience to God and homage to His election.

mots.racine: س ج د / م ل أ
ك ل ل / ج م ع

versets.ar: إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ
versets.tr: illā iblīsa stakbara wa-kāna mina l-kāfirīna
versets.trad: save Iblīs, who waxed proud and was among the deniers.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: the wretchedness of Iblīs was revealed, and by his refusal he fell under the curse. Pride (istikbār) is the root of his fall: before the dignity of Adam made manifest, his heart reared up, and there he stands ranked among the deniers. The contrast with the unanimous prostration of the angels underscores that the loss comes from the gaze upon self alone.

mots.def: Iblīs: the devil, the rejected one.

versets.ar: قَالَ يَا إِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ ۖ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ الْعَالِينَ
versets.tr: qāla yā iblīsu mā manaʿaka an tasjuda li-mā khalaqtu bi-yadayya astakbarta am kunta mina l-ʿālīna
versets.trad: God said: “O Iblīs! What kept you from prostrating before the one I created with My hands? Have you waxed proud, or are you among the exalted?”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “created with My hands” tells of Adam’s singular favour — what God deposited in Adam is found in no other; in him appeared the specificity (khuṣūṣiyya). The question “have you waxed proud?” already discloses the cause of the fault: the illusion of superiority, the gaze cast upon self rather than upon the divine command.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / ي د ي
mots.sens: whom I created with My hands

versets.ar: قَالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ خَلَقْتَنِي مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِن طِينٍ
versets.tr: qāla anā khayrun minhu khalaqtanī min nārin wa-khalaqtahu min ṭīnin
versets.trad: He said: “I am better than he: You created me from fire, and You created him from clay.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unmasks the sophism of Iblīs: from this came his error — he imagined that superiority lies in matter and substance, ignorant that it lies in apportionment and election (al-qisma), not in composition (al-khilqa). To measure worth by fire rather than clay is to confound the nobility of the being with that of its loam.

mots.racine: ن و ر / ط ي ن

versets.ar: قَالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ
versets.tr: qāla fa-khruj minhā fa-innaka rajīmun
versets.trad: God said: “Go forth from here, for you are an outcast!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “go forth” — from the Garden, from the form that was yours, from the state in which you were; “for you are stoned” (rajīm): struck by My curse, by the shooting stars of heaven, and by the projectiles loosed from the hearts of the saints if you assail them. The expulsion is at once spatial and ontological: Iblīs falls from his whole rank.

versets.ar: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ
versets.tr: wa-inna ʿalayka laʿnatī ilā yawmi d-dīni
versets.trad: My curse shall be upon you until the Day of final Reckoning.”
versets.commentaire: The divine curse (laʿna) weighs upon Iblīs “until the Day of Recompense” (yawm ad-dīn). Qushayrī inscribes in this sentence the seal of degradation: having refused the humble prostration, the rejected one henceforth bears the weight of estrangement until the end of time.

versets.ar: قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِي إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ
versets.tr: qāla rabbi fa-anẓirnī ilā yawmi yubʿathūna
versets.trad: He said: “My Lord! Grant me respite until the day when men are raised up.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī sees in this request the height of degradation: at the completion of his wretchedness, he asked for his own reprieve — his tongue uttered it, his will fastened on demanding the delay, thereby increasing until the Resurrection the cause of his chastisement. The respite he seeks is but a prolonging of his perdition.

mots.sens: the day they are raised up
mots.def: baʿatha: to raise from the dead.

versets.ar: قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ الْمُنظَرِينَ
versets.tr: qāla fa-innaka mina l-munẓarīna
versets.trad: God said: “Yes, you are among those granted respite
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God grants him the delay, for it is by his own tongue that he asked for the completion of his wretchedness. The respite is not a favour but an answer to his request: Iblīs obtains the time he demanded, and that very time becomes the space of his damnation.

mots.def: munẓar: to whom a reprieve is granted.

versets.ar: إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ
versets.tr: ilā yawmi l-waqti l-maʿlūmi
versets.trad: until the day of the appointed time.”
versets.commentaire: The respite of Iblīs ends “on the day of the known time” (al-waqt al-maʿlūm). Qushayrī recalls that this term is fixed in the divine knowledge: the reprieve has a bound, and no creature, were it the rejected one, escapes the decree that seals its hour.

versets.ar: قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: qāla fa-bi-ʿizzatika la-ughwiyannahum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: Iblīs said: “By Your might! I shall lead them all astray,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī notes the paradox: had he truly known His might (ʿizza), he would not have sworn by it to disobey Him. Iblīs invokes the very attribute of God to announce his rebellion — proof that he knows the divine majesty without submitting to it, and that his knowledge has not saved him.

mots.sens: I shall surely lead them astray
mots.def: aghwā: to lead into error, to seduce.

versets.ar: إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ
versets.tr: illā ʿibādaka minhumu l-mukhlaṣīna
versets.trad: save Your devoted servants.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: Iblīs himself acknowledges that he has no power over “Your purified servants” (al-mukhlaṣīn) — those whom God has set apart and made pure. The elect sincerity (ikhlāṣ), gift of God and not conquest of man, is the inviolable rampart against the seduction of the tempter.

mots.def: mukhlaṣ: whom God has made pure, elect.

versets.ar: قَالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ
versets.tr: qāla fa-l-ḥaqqu wa-l-ḥaqqa aqūlu
versets.trad: God said: “So it shall be, and the truth is what I speak!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: God swears by the True (al-Ḥaqq) and declares that He speaks only the truth. He notes elsewhere that the effrontery of Iblīs in addressing the True — persisting in opposition and swearing to disobey — is uglier and more deserving of the curse than his initial refusal to prostrate. Obstinacy in the fault surpasses the fault itself.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ق و ل

versets.ar: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ
versets.tr: la-amlaʾanna jahannama minka wa-mimman tabiʿaka minhum ajmaʿīna
versets.trad: I shall fill Gehenna with you and with all who follow you.”
versets.commentaire: The divine oath seals the lot of Iblīs and his following: “I shall fill Gehenna with you and with all who follow you.” Qushayrī reads in it the fulfilment of the sentence: the rebellion of the tempter and the adherence of his followers lead to the same abode, by a justice that does not lie.

versets.ar: قُلْ مَا أَسْأَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ
versets.tr: qul mā asʾalukum ʿalayhi min ajrin wa-mā anā mina l-mutakallifīna
versets.trad: Say [O Muḥammad]: “I ask of you no reward for this, and I am not of those who arrogate to themselves a mission.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī, sealing the sūra by the address to the Prophet: I have not come of myself nor by my own choosing; I have only been sent to you. The messenger asks no reward and arrogates (takalluf) to himself no mission of his own accord: he conveys what he has received, disinterested and submitted.

mots.racine: س أ ل / أ ج ر
mots.sens: I ask of you no reward

mots.def: mutakallif: who arrogates what is not his.

versets.ar: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: in huwa illā dhikrun li-l-ʿālamīna
versets.trad: This is but a Reminder addressed to the worlds,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “this is but a Reminder for the worlds” — it is the Qurʾān, an exhortation (ʿiẓa) for you. The message overflows all particularity: it is dhikr — remembrance and warning — destined for the whole of creatures, and not for the Prophet’s people alone.

versets.ar: وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُ بَعْدَ حِينٍ
versets.tr: wa-la-taʿlamunna nabaʾahu baʿda ḥīnin
versets.trad: and you shall surely know the truth of it after a time.”
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī closes upon a proof through duration: you shall know its truth after a time, when its Law has held fast — for such a thing, were it false, would not endure. The very permanence of the Revelation, traversing the ages, attests its truth: the abiding of the Law and its perpetuity are signs of its authenticity.

mots.racine: ع ل م / ن ب أ
mots.sens: you shall surely know its tiding
